payload.audience: application developers using dali-ui public APIs

content_quality.default_object_model: Use Dali::Ui::View rather than Dali::Actor for app-facing object examples.
content_quality.guide_assumption: The reader is using dali-ui to build applications.

code_verification: receiver method exists on the receiver type
static methods are called statically and member methods are called on valid instances
assignment target is compatible with the method return type
argument count, argument type, and overload shape match the public API
enum owner and enum value both exist
property constants use the correct owner and value type
signal callback signatures match the public signal typedef
code blocks do not rely on inferred namespaces or overloads from naming alone
draft_requirements: Write enough practical detail for application developers to use the feature in a dali-ui app.
Ground natural-language behavior claims in the actual public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when the context pack is not enough.
Before writing examples, inspect the referenced public headers and relevant sample files when needed.
Prefer examples copied or adapted from existing samples when they match the feature; otherwise keep examples small and verify every API use.
After drafting, review the completed Markdown line by line against repository evidence and fix inaccurate explanations or code before returning.
repair_requirements: Repair only the problematic spans and the smallest surrounding context needed; do not rewrite the whole document.
For each listed issue, use the repository evidence and context pack to verify the corrected wording or code before returning.
If an issue reveals the same API misuse nearby, fix the nearby equivalent misuse too; do not make unrelated style rewrites.
After repair, re-check the edited lines against public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when needed.

rules: Use dali-ui symbols as the app-facing API surface.
Use dali-core and dali-adaptor symbols only as setup/background unless the usage pattern explicitly marks them preferred.
Prefer View-based examples over raw Actor object-model examples.

rendered_prompt:
You are reviewing an existing DALi dali-ui application developer guide draft.
This is Phase 2.3.5: source-grounded prose review.

Primary goal:
- Improve natural-language accuracy and guide flow using repository evidence.
- Preserve the document's useful structure and examples whenever possible.
- Do not perform a full rewrite.
- Keep the revised user-facing guide in en only.
- Remove Korean, Japanese, Chinese, or other non-English prose from the revised guide.

Feature target:
- Feature: Addon Manager (addon-manager)
- Category: platform-integration
- Guide profile: dali-ui-app
- Audience: application developers using dali-ui public APIs

Review rules:
- Review every natural-language paragraph line by line against public headers, implementation files, samples, and UTCs when needed.
- Accuracy is the first priority. Flow is the second priority.
- Edit the smallest unit needed: word, sentence, paragraph, or section-local addition/removal.
- If a word-level change is enough, change only the word.
- If a sentence-level change is enough, change only the sentence.
- If an entire paragraph is based on a wrong premise, revise, add, or remove that paragraph.
- Preserve code blocks when possible.
- If prose can be corrected to match an already accurate code block, prefer changing prose.
- If the code block itself makes the prose inaccurate or breaks the guide flow, rewrite only the smallest necessary part of that code block and verify it against public headers/source.
- Do not invent APIs.
- Do not add generic filler just to make the document longer.
- Do not change section structure unless prose_review.allow_section_restructure is true or the current prose is clearly misleading.
- Prefer localized edits when prose_review.localized_edits_only is true.
- Preserve code blocks especially when prose_review.preserve_code_blocks is true.
- Prefer Dali::Ui::View, typed setters, fluent chaining, and view-tree idioms for app-facing guidance.
- Treat dali-core and dali-adaptor APIs as reference/context unless the feature explicitly requires them.

Original Markdown draft:
---
title: Addon Manager
sidebar_label: Addon Manager
category: platform-integration
---

# Addon Manager

Addon Manager lets a dali-ui application discover add-ons, load them, inspect their metadata, call exported procedures, and forward application lifecycle events.

## Table of Contents

- [Accessing the Addon Manager](#accessing-the-addon-manager)
- [Discovering and Inspecting Add-ons](#discovering-and-inspecting-add-ons)
- [Loading Add-ons and Calling Procedures](#loading-add-ons-and-calling-procedures)
- [Coordinating Add-on Lifecycle](#coordinating-add-on-lifecycle)
- [Registering Built-in Dispatch Tables](#registering-built-in-dispatch-tables)

## Accessing the Addon Manager

Use `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get` to access the active manager instance. In a dali-ui application, keep add-on integration separate from your `Dali::Ui::View` tree: views own application UI, while `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager` owns add-on discovery, loading, procedure lookup, and lifecycle forwarding.

```cpp
void InitializePlatformAddOns()
{
  Dali::Integration::AddOnManager* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get();

  if(!manager)
  {
    return;
  }

  manager->Start();
}
```

`Dali::Integration::AddOnManager` is the app-facing entry point for this feature. The constructor `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::AddOnManager` and destructor `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::~AddOnManager` are part of the manager type, but application code normally works with the singleton returned by `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get`.

## Discovering and Inspecting Add-ons

Call `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::EnumerateAddOns` to list add-on names. For a specific add-on, `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::GetAddOnInfo` fills a `Dali::AddOnInfo` record containing `Dali::AddOnInfo::name`, `Dali::AddOnInfo::type`, `Dali::AddOnInfo::version`, `Dali::AddOnInfo::buildInfo`, and `Dali::AddOnInfo::next`.

The nested `Dali::AddOnInfo::BuildInfo` record exposes build compatibility fields through `Dali::AddOnInfo::BuildInfo::libCoreVersion`, `Dali::AddOnInfo::BuildInfo::libAdaptorVersion`, and `Dali::AddOnInfo::BuildInfo::libToolkitVersion`.

```cpp
void PrintAvailableAddOns()
{
  Dali::Integration::AddOnManager* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get();

  if(!manager)
  {
    return;
  }

  const std::vector<std::string> addOnNames = manager->EnumerateAddOns();

  for(const std::string& addOnName : addOnNames)
  {
    Dali::AddOnInfo info{};

    if(manager->GetAddOnInfo(addOnName, info))
    {
      std::cout << "Add-on: " << info.name << "\n";
      std::cout << "Type: " << info.type << "\n";
      std::cout << "Version: " << info.version << "\n";
      std::cout << "Core: " << info.buildInfo.libCoreVersion << "\n";
      std::cout << "Adaptor: " << info.buildInfo.libAdaptorVersion << "\n";
      std::cout << "Toolkit: " << info.buildInfo.libToolkitVersion << "\n";
    }
  }
}
```

Use `Dali::AddOnInfo::next` when add-on metadata is provided as a linked sequence. For most application-level checks, read the current `Dali::AddOnInfo` entry and avoid depending on add-ons that are not present in `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::EnumerateAddOns`.

## Loading Add-ons and Calling Procedures

Use `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::LoadAddOn` when you know both the add-on name and library name. Use `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::LoadAddOns` when you want to load several known add-ons from their names.

```cpp
void LoadRequiredAddOns()
{
  Dali::Integration::AddOnManager* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get();

  if(!manager)
  {
    return;
  }

  auto imageAddOn = manager->LoadAddOn("image-loader", "libimage-loader-addon.so");

  std::vector<std::string> optionalAddOns;
  optionalAddOns.push_back("metrics");
  optionalAddOns.push_back("diagnostics");

  const auto loadedAddOns = manager->LoadAddOns(optionalAddOns);

  std::cout << "Loaded optional add-ons: " << loadedAddOns.size() << "\n";
}
```

After an add-on is loaded, use `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::GetGlobalProc` to retrieve a typed callable for a global exported procedure. The template argument is the function signature you expect from the add-on.

```cpp
void CallGlobalAddOnFunction()
{
  Dali::Integration::AddOnManager* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get();

  if(!manager)
  {
    return;
  }

  auto addOn = manager->LoadAddOn("diagnostics", "libdiagnostics-addon.so");

  auto getValue = manager->GetGlobalProc<int()>(addOn, "GetDiagnosticsValue");

  if(getValue)
  {
    const int value = getValue();
    std::cout << "Diagnostics value: " << value << "\n";
  }
}
```

For direct one-shot calls, `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::InvokeGlobalProc` loads the exported procedure by name and invokes it with the supplied arguments.

```cpp
int ReadAddOnCounter()
{
  Dali::Integration::AddOnManager* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get();

  if(!manager)
  {
    return 0;
  }

  auto addOn = manager->LoadAddOn("counter", "libcounter-addon.so");

  return manager->InvokeGlobalProc<int>(addOn, "ReadCounter");
}
```

Use `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::GetInstanceProc` when an add-on exposes a procedure that operates on an instance pointer or handle supplied by the add-on integration layer.

```cpp
void ResetAddOnInstance(void* instance)
{
  Dali::Integration::AddOnManager* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get();

  if(!manager || !instance)
  {
    return;
  }

  auto addOn = manager->LoadAddOn("counter", "libcounter-addon.so");

  auto reset = manager->GetInstanceProc<void(void*)>(addOn, "ResetCounterInstance");

  if(reset)
  {
    reset(instance);
  }
}
```

## Coordinating Add-on Lifecycle

Forward application state changes to the add-on system with `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Start`, `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Pause`, `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Resume`, and `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Stop`.

This keeps add-on lifecycle handling aligned with the surrounding dali-ui application while your `Dali::Ui::View` hierarchy remains focused on presentation and interaction.

```cpp
class AddOnLifecycleController
{
public:
  void OnApplicationStarted()
  {
    if(auto* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get())
    {
      manager->Start();
    }
  }

  void OnApplicationPaused()
  {
    if(auto* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get())
    {
      manager->Pause();
    }
  }

  void OnApplicationResumed()
  {
    if(auto* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get())
    {
      manager->Resume();
    }
  }

  void OnApplicationStopped()
  {
    if(auto* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get())
    {
      manager->Stop();
    }
  }
};
```

The lifecycle calls are manager-level operations. Add-ons that provide dispatch tables can respond through `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable::OnStart`, `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable::OnPause`, `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable::OnResume`, and `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable::OnStop`.

## Registering Built-in Dispatch Tables

Some integrations expose add-ons through a `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable` instead of loading only from a shared library. Register that table with `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::RegisterAddOnDispatchTable`.

A `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable` identifies the add-on with `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable::name`, provides metadata through `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable::GetAddOnInfo`, exposes global procedures through `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable::GetGlobalProc`, exposes instance procedures through `Dali::AddOnDispatchTable::GetInstanceProc`, and receives lifecycle callbacks through its `OnStart`, `OnPause`, `OnResume`, and `OnStop` entries.

```cpp
void RegisterBuiltInAddOn(const Dali::AddOnDispatchTable* dispatchTable)
{
  if(!dispatchTable)
  {
    return;
  }

  Dali::Integration::AddOnManager* manager = Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Get();

  if(!manager)
  {
    return;
  }

  manager->RegisterAddOnDispatchTable(dispatchTable);
}
```

Once registered, a built-in dispatch table participates in the same manager workflow as other add-ons: applications can discover metadata with `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::GetAddOnInfo`, obtain callable procedures with `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::GetGlobalProc` or `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::GetInstanceProc`, and forward lifecycle events through `Dali::Integration::AddOnManager::Start`, `Pause`, `Resume`, and `Stop`.
